steps.title: Interpret as a quadratic in z to the ninth power and set up factorization
steps.content:
The expression $34z^{18} + bz^9 + 30$ is a quadratic in $z^9$. If $qz^9 + r$ is a factor, then: $$34z^{18} + bz^9 + 30 = (qz^9 + r)(az^9 + c)$$
for positive integers $a$ and $c$.

steps.title: Expand and match coefficients to find constraints on b
steps.content:
Expanding the right side: $$(qz^9 + r)(az^9 + c) = qaz^{18} + (qc + ra)z^9 + rc$$ Matching with the original expression:
  • $z^{18}$: $qa = 34$
  • $z^9$: $qc + ra = b$
  • Constant: $rc = 30$
Therefore $b = qc + ra$, where $(q, a)$ and $(r, c)$ are factor pairs of 34 and 30 respectively.

steps.title: Maximize b by testing extreme factor pairings
steps.content:
To maximize $b = qc + ra$, test divisor pairings at the extremes.

When $(q, a) = (1, 34)$ and $(r, c) = (30, 1)$: $$b = 1 \cdot 1 + 30 \cdot 34 = 1 + 1020 = 1021$$ When $(q, a) = (34, 1)$ and $(r, c) = (1, 30)$: $$b = 34 \cdot 30 + 1 \cdot 1 = 1020 + 1 = 1021$$ Other factor pairs (such as $(2, 17)$ paired with divisors of 30) produce smaller sums.

The greatest possible value of $b$ is 1021.
